items.d: A woman in scary stories who can do magic.
items.s: Do all ___ wear funny hats? — Yes, and they can do magic, too!
items.hintDe: Denk an Besen, spitzen Hut und Zauberei.
items.note: WB Using Vocabulary ex.2(3): 'Do all witches wear funny hats?' verbatim; reply adapted ('they can fly' -> 'they can do magic') because 'fly' is above level.

items.d: A dead man or woman who can come back at night in scary stories.
items.s: Edwina was dressed like a girl from the 19th century. Then she disappeared. Was she a ___?
items.hintDe: Weiß und durchsichtig — buh!
items.note: Stitched from the SB story 'The mysterious girl': 'She was dressed like a girl from the 19th century.' + 'And then she disappeared.' — closing question added.

items.d: Children carry it when they go trick-or-treating and put their sweets in it.
items.s: The child is trick-or-treating with a ___ for the sweets.
items.hintDe: Da kommen deine Süßigkeiten rein.
items.note: No textbook running-text sentence exists for this word; carrier modeled on the SB p.28 Word File image (child trick-or-treating with a pumpkin bucket).

items.d: In scary stories, a man with long teeth and a black cape. At Halloween, many children dress like him.
items.s: I've got my ___ costume, but I can't find my teeth.
items.hintDe: Er hat lange, spitze Zähne.
items.note: WB Using Vocabulary ex.2(8) minimally adapted: above-level 'plastic' dropped from 'my plastic teeth'.

items.d: At Halloween, children knock on doors and ask for sweets.
items.s: When my friends meet for ___, we go from door to door.
items.hintDe: Von Tür zu Tür ziehen und Süßigkeiten sammeln.
items.note: First clause verbatim from the SB poem 'I'm not so keen on Halloween' ('When my friends meet for trick or treat'); in-bank completion added for level and disambiguation.

items.d: You play this at Halloween: there is food in water, and you must catch it with your mouth — no fingers!
items.s: We also play ___ at Halloween. It's difficult, but fun — you must catch an apple with your mouth!
items.hintDe: Ein Partyspiel: Obst fangen — nur mit dem Mund!
items.note: Stitched from Megan's SB webpage text ('And we also play apple bobbing.', 'It's difficult, but fun.'); apple/mouth clause added in-bank to make the blank unambiguous.

items.d: You wear it over your eyes and nose, so you look like a ghost or a vampire.
items.s: Everybody wears a ___ over their eyes. We're vampires, witches and ghosts!
items.hintDe: Du trägst sie im Gesicht.
items.note: Megan's SB sentence 'Everyone wears a mask.' — 'everyone' is above level, so 'everybody'; 'over their eyes' added to rule out 'costume'. Second sentence verbatim Megan.

items.d: What a family always does, year after year — at Halloween or on a birthday.
items.s: What's your favourite Halloween ___ — apple bobbing or trick or treat?
items.hintDe: Etwas, das ihr jedes Jahr wieder macht.
items.note: Masterlist sentence verbatim; two in-bank example traditions appended so 'costume' no longer fits the blank.

items.d: To be scared of a ghost, a big dog or a scary story.
items.s: We do not ___ the zombies, because we aren't scared of them!
items.hintDe: Du hast große Angst vor etwas.
items.note: Masterlist sentence (SB song line) verbatim; because-clause added so 'scare' cannot fill the blank; 'zombies' kept from the song and glossed.

items.d: To make a piece go away with a knife
items.s: Please give me the knife. I want to ___ the top of the pumpkin.
items.hintDe: Das machst du mit einem Messer – zum Beispiel beim Kürbis.
items.note: WB ex. 2.7 dialogue chosen over the masterlist example: the knife context makes the verb unambiguous. The 'What for?' turn was dropped. Glosses: top, pumpkin (both out-of-bank; only 'pumpkin bucket' is taught).

items.d: The glass in a building that you look out of, into the street
items.s: We put some vampire stickers on his ___.
items.hintDe: Das Glas vorne am Haus – da siehst du auf die Straße.
items.note: Masterlist example verbatim (= Steve's SB webpage answer). 'door'/'car'/'window' deliberately kept out of mc/pool: they would also fit the carrier.

items.d: To have a thing and not give it away
items.s: Let's ___ the pumpkin for Halloween.
items.hintDe: Du gibst es nicht mehr her – es bleibt bei dir.
items.note: Masterlist example shortened: 'for our Halloween party' → 'for Halloween' (bare 'party' is out-of-bank; only 'surprise party' is taught). Gloss: pumpkin.

items.d: When you are very happy about a good thing that you did
items.s: This year my pumpkin was the best. I was very ___ of it.
items.hintDe: Dein Kürbis ist der beste in der Klasse – wie fühlst du dich?
items.note: Henry's SB text, minimally adapted: 'pumpkin face' → 'pumpkin' ('face' is out-of-bank) and '+ of it' to anchor the preposition. The masterlist sentence alone ('I was very proud.') is a bad carrier: happy/scared/excited also fit.

items.d: In a building, you go up these to the rooms over you
items.s: We go up the ___ in the dark.
items.hintDe: Da gehst du Stufe für Stufe nach oben.
items.note: Masterlist example with walk → go: bare 'walk' is out-of-bank (only phrase entries like 'to walk on' are taught). 'street'/'road' kept out of mc/pool: they would also fit 'up the ___ in the dark'.

items.d: A small picture that you can put on a window, a book or a door
items.s: I put a vampire ___ on his window.
items.hintDe: Kleine bunte Bilder für dein Heft oder dein Fenster.

items.d: Food with a lot of sugar in it, like chocolate
items.s: People sometimes give us ___ at Halloween.
items.hintDe: Die sammelst du an Halloween im Kürbiseimer – lecker, aber schlecht für die Zähne!
items.note: Masterlist example ('Children get sweets for Halloween.') uses bare 'get', which is out-of-bank (only 'to get back' etc. are taught). Switched to Steve's SB sentence 'People sometimes give us a treat – sweets, etc.', simplified ('a treat' is out-of-bank).

items.d: Children ask this at the door at Halloween when they want sweets
items.s: We knocked on the door. A woman opened it. "___?" Darren asked.
items.hintDe: Diesen Ruf hörst du an Halloween an jeder Haustür.
items.note: Fixed doorstep exclamation (distinct from the wordfile activity entry). Carrier from the WB ex. 11 story ('"Trick or treat?" Darren asked.'), trimmed: 'walked up to the front door' and 'old' dropped — walk/front/old are out-of-bank as bare tokens. The masterlist example needs 'people's' and 'say' (both out-of-bank).

items.d: With this, you can cut off a piece of bread or cheese
items.s: I need a ___ to cut vegetables.
items.hintDe: Vorsicht, scharf! Damit schneidest du dein Brot.
items.note: Masterlist example verbatim; bare 'cut' is out-of-bank (only the phrase 'cut off' is taught), so it carries a gloss. 'knives' is full in deToEn because German 'Messer' is singular and plural. The d recycles in-bank 'cut off'.

items.d: One hundred years - a very long time.
items.s: She was dressed like a girl from the 19th ___.
items.hintDe: Denk an eine sehr lange Zeit: hundert Jahre.
items.note: v1 carrier invented untaught words (carols etc.); replaced by the masterlist sentence, which also appears verbatim in the SB story. 'the 19th ___' pins century - no other time noun works after an ordinal. All distractors are in-bank time nouns, unlike v1's decade/millennium.

items.d: You wear it at Halloween to look like a vampire, a witch or a ghost.
items.s: Her ___ is scary! She looks like a vampire.
items.hintDe: Das ziehst du zu Halloween an.
items.note: Masterlist sentence with 'looks great'/'real' rephrased to 'is scary' ('great' and 'real' are not in the level gate). mask and dress also fit the blank, so they are accepted as partial and kept out of MC; distractors are everyday clothing that cannot make you look like a vampire.

items.d: You wanted to do it yesterday, but it was not possible.
items.s: Lara wanted to eat more sweets, but she ___ - she was too sick.
items.hintDe: Du wolltest es tun, aber es ging einfach nicht.
items.note: Masterlist 'Laura couldn't stop.' is too bare as a blank (many auxiliaries fit) and 'stop' is untaught; carrier rebuilt from the SB story beat (Lara, too many sweets, sick) so past inability is the only sensible reading. 'didn't' stays grammatical, hence partial and excluded from MC; 'can't' is tense-blocked and makes a clean distractor.

items.d: Small, pretty and nice to look at - like a young rabbit.
items.s: Look at my rabbit. Isn't it ___? I love its small nose and big eyes.
items.hintDe: Denk an ein junges Häschen - einfach zum Liebhaben.
items.note: Masterlist sentence with cat swapped for rabbit ('cat' is not in the level gate, g1-u09 pets list has rabbit) and a third sentence added: bare 'Isn't it ___?' also accepts big, small, wild etc. The nose-and-eyes cue narrows it; pretty/beautiful/sweet remain defensible, so partial and excluded from MC. Definition adapted from the v1 seed after a token gate check.

items.d: Girls often wear it to a party - it is long, like a skirt and a shirt in one.
items.s: Edwina wears a long ___, like a girl from the 19th century.
items.hintDe: Mädchen tragen es auf Partys - wie Rock und Oberteil in einem.
items.note: Masterlist carrier ('The ___ is beautiful. You can wear it to the party.') accepts mask, costume, skirt, blouse and more once blanked; rebuilt around the SB story image (Edwina, the girl from the 19th century, SB ex. 6 'The girl's dress looked very old'). skirt/costume stay grammatical, hence partial and kept out of MC.

items.d: A dark place with old stones where the dead lie.
items.s: There were some trees next to the ___, a dark place with old stones for the dead.
items.hintDe: Ein dunkler Ort mit alten Grabsteinen.
items.note: First clause verbatim from the SB story ('There were some trees next to the graveyard'); defining clause added because the bare sentence accepts park, woods, river and most other place nouns. 'stones for the dead' rules out castle, which then works as the Halloween-flavoured distractor.

items.d: Me, and no one else - like in: "I looked at ... in the mirror."
items.s: When I am alone, I often talk to ___.
items.hintDe: Denk dran: Ich rede mit mir - nicht mit ihm oder ihr.
items.note: Masterlist sentence with 'When I am alone,' added: bare 'I often talk to ___.' accepts him/her/them/you. The alone-context makes every other pronoun clearly wrong, so the digest pronouns work as distractors (v1 used himself/herself/yourself - all out of bank). Definition uses a mirror frame instead of meta-talk; 'word for me' fails the gate ('word' untaught).

items.d: You ask a friend about what to do: "Where ... we go? What ... we do?"
items.s: "Where ___ we go?" asked Edwina. "Well, down the road!"
items.hintDe: Du machst einen Vorschlag: Wohin ... wir gehen?
items.note: SB story line ('"Where shall we go?" she asked.') with she -> Edwina and Ron's reply kept to anchor the suggestion meaning. should/can stay defensible in the question, so partial and excluded from MC; must/did/couldn't are clearly wrong in a friendly where-question. Difficulty 3: abstract function word, two German senses (sollen/wollen).

items.d: Ill and not well. You can be like this after too many sweets.
items.s: I feel a bit ___. I ate too many sweets.
items.hintDe: Zu viele Süßigkeiten – und dir geht es nicht gut.
items.note: Masterlist sentence verbatim; 'feel' and 'a bit' are not in the cumulative bank, so both are glossed (both appear in the SB story this sentence comes from).

items.d: A very strong woman from a story or film. She helps everybody and she can often fly.
items.s: She was dressed up as her favourite ___.
items.hintDe: Denk an starke Frauen aus Comics, die fliegen können.
items.note: Masterlist verbatim; 'dressed up' glossed exactly as the SB story glosses it (dressed up – verkleidet). MC distractors are abstract nouns on purpose: in 'dressed up as her favourite ___' any person noun (witch, vampire, ghost) is a plausible costume and so not unambiguously wrong.

items.d: Very noisy and not careful, with a lot of running and shouting.
items.s: You shouldn't play many ___ games.
items.hintDe: Denk an laute Spiele, bei denen alle herumrennen.
items.note: Masterlist verbatim. should/shouldn't is this unit's grammar focus but is absent from the vocab level gate (only 'shall' and 'couldn't' are banked), so 'shouldn't' carries a gloss to pass the deterministic check; students know it from the grammar section.

items.d: To make somebody jump with fear, like a ghost at Halloween.
items.s: At Halloween, some costumes ___ me.
items.hintDe: Das machen gruselige Kostüme zu Halloween mit dir.
items.note: Masterlist with 'really' dropped ('really' is not in the level gate); otherwise verbatim. 'fear' as MC distractor targets the classic scare/fear confusion (make afraid vs be afraid).

items.d: A strong hat that you wear when you ride your bike.
items.s: Always wear a ___ when you ride your bike.
items.hintDe: Der schützt deinen Kopf beim Radfahren.
items.note: Masterlist carrier ('You should always wear a cycle helmet.') loses all disambiguating context once blanked, and 'should' is not in the level gate; rebuilt with taught words plus bike context (v1 carrier shape, verified token by token).

items.d: A name for a group of friends or children.
items.s: Come on, ___. Don't look. Promise.
items.hintDe: So nennst du eine ganze Gruppe von Freunden.
items.note: Masterlist verbatim (Twins 1 video phrase). Vocative carrier, so MC distractors are plural person nouns that are unidiomatic as a friendly address.

items.d: To not have something any more because you cannot find it.
items.s: I don't want to ___ my phone.
items.hintDe: Das Gegenteil von finden – denk an dein Handy.
items.note: Masterlist verbatim. Definition adapted from the v1 seed after a token-by-token gate check (all words taught). 'break' and 'sell' would be true in this sentence, so they are accepted/avoided accordingly, never used as distractors.

items.d: Lunch outside. You sit in the park and eat sandwiches from a basket.
items.s: Let's have a ___ in the park. I can bring sandwiches and orange juice.
items.hintDe: Du isst draußen im Park – mit Korb und Decke.
items.note: First sentence is masterlist verbatim; second sentence added because 'Let's have a ___ in the park.' alone also accepts party, match, concert and break from the bank. Event/meal nouns (party, sandwich, concert, match, supper) are deliberately excluded from MC as they would be defensible answers.
